summary: OpenAI cutting Codex pricing signals the endgame is approaching; coding tokens are so valuable that labs must acquire them at scale to maintain RSI feedback loops
summary_extended: Baker argues that OpenAI's decision to cut Codex pricing is not a conventional competitive move but a strategic land-grab for coding tokens at scale, which he views as critical inputs to recursive self-improvement (RSI) feedback loops. His reasoning is that coding tokens occupy a uniquely privileged role in AI model development — they are the substrate through which models improve their own code-writing and reasoning capabilities — and that labs which fail to accumulate sufficient coding token volume may be permanently locked out of the RSI loop entirely. Baker interprets the urgency of this pricing behavior as a signal that the major AI labs believe the window to enter RSI dynamics is closing, framing the current moment as an 'endgame' phase of the foundational AI race. The investment implication is directional but difficult to act on directly, as the primary actors (OpenAI, Anthropic) are private; no specific publicly traded beneficiary is unambiguously implied by this thesis alone.
detail: Baker interprets OpenAI's Codex price cuts as a strategic move to acquire coding tokens at scale, because coding tokens are critical inputs to recursive self-improvement (RSI) loops. If you don't have enough coding tokens, you may not be able to enter the RSI loop. This is why everyone is behaving like the endgame is near.
confidence_evidence: Baker frames this as his interpretation/inference rather than stated fact; uses 'I think' framing
quote_fragment: coding tokens are so valuable and if you don't have enough you may not get into this RSI loop
